bibliographic_data.title: M3PT: A Transformer for Multimodal, Multi-Party Social Signal Prediction with Person-aware Blockwise Attention
bibliographic_data.abstract: Understanding social signals in multi-party conversations is important for human-robot interaction and artificial social intelligence. Social signals include body pose, head pose, speech, and context-specific activities like acquiring and taking bites of food when dining. Past work in multi-party interaction tends to build task-specific models for predicting social signals. In this work, we address the challenge of predicting multimodal social signals in multi-party settings in a single model. We introduce M3PT, a causal transformer architecture with modality and temporal blockwise attention masking to simultaneously process multiple social cues across multiple participants and their temporal interactions.

core_content.novelty_claim: M3PT introduces a causal transformer architecture with modality and temporal blockwise attention masking to predict multimodal social signals in multi-party settings.
core_content.key_methodology: M3PT uses a VQ-VAE for tokenizing continuous inputs like gaze, head pose, or pose into quantized keypoint representations. It applies person-aware and modality-aware blockwise attention masking to attend over these tokens across modality, time, and interlocutor.
outcomes: M3PT improves bite timing and speaking status prediction.
Using multiple modalities enhances the model's performance.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Understanding social signals in multi-party conversations is crucial for human-robot interaction and artificial social intelligence. Social signals include body pose, head pose, speech, and context-specific activities like acquiring and taking bites of food when dining. Past work often focuses on building task-specific models to predict these signals. This paper introduces M3PT, a causal transformer architecture that can simultaneously process multiple social cues across participants in multi-party settings. The model leverages modality and temporal blockwise attention masking to learn the relationships between different social signals over time. It is trained on the Human-Human Commensality Dataset (HHCD) and demonstrates improved bite timing and speaking status prediction when using multiple modalities.
